section.title: Benjamin's Allotment
section.sectionRationale: The nation gathers at Shiloh and sets up the Tent of Meeting, and Joshua confronts the seven tribes who have not yet taken their inheritance: how long will you put off possessing the land the LORD has given you? The land is surveyed, divided into seven portions, and distributed by lot before the LORD, beginning with Benjamin. It is one movement, the renewed distribution at Shiloh, which rebukes spiritual slackness and calls God's people to actively enter what He has freely given.
section.thesis: God has given the land, yet seven tribes hang back from possessing it, so Joshua rebukes their delay and organizes a careful, worshipful distribution by lot at the sanctuary, teaching that the sure gift of God is not to be neglected but entered, and Benjamin, small but strategically placed, receives its portion first.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the assembly at Shiloh and Joshua's rebuke of delay, verses 1 to 7; the survey and casting of lots before the LORD, verses 8 to 10; and Benjamin's borders and cities, verses 11 to 28.

units.label: The assembly at Shiloh and Joshua's rebuke
units.note: how long will you put off possessing the land

verses.text: Then the whole congregation of Israel assembled at Shiloh and set up the Tent of Meeting there. And though the land was subdued before them,
there were still seven tribes of Israel who had not yet received their inheritance.
So Joshua said to the Israelites, “How long will you {{rafah|put off}} entering and possessing the land that the LORD, the God of your fathers, has given you?
Appoint three men from each tribe, and I will send them out to survey the land and map it out, according to the inheritance of each. Then they will return to me
and divide the land into seven portions. Judah shall remain in their territory in the south, and the house of Joseph shall remain in their territory in the north.
When you have mapped out the seven portions of land and brought it to me, I will cast {{goral|lots}} for you here in the presence of the LORD our God.

The Levites, however, have no portion among you, because their inheritance is the priesthood of the LORD. And Gad, Reuben, and half the tribe of Manasseh have already received the inheritance that Moses the servant of the LORD gave them beyond the Jordan to the east.”

units.label: The survey and casting of lots before the LORD
units.note: Joshua cast lots in the presence of the LORD at Shiloh

verses.text: As the men got up to go out, Joshua commanded them to map out the land, saying, “Go and survey the land, map it out, and return to me. Then I will cast lots for you here in Shiloh in the presence of the LORD.”
So the men departed and went throughout the land, mapping it city by city into seven portions. Then they returned with the document to Joshua at the camp in Shiloh.
And Joshua cast lots for them in the presence of the LORD at Shiloh, where he distributed the land to the Israelites according to their divisions.

units.label: Benjamin's borders and cities
units.note: the first lot came up for Benjamin

verses.text: The first lot came up for the clans of the tribe of Benjamin. Their allotted territory lay between the tribes of Judah and Joseph:
On the north side their border began at the Jordan, went up past the northern slope of Jericho, headed west through the hill country, and came out at the wilderness of Beth-aven.
From there the border crossed over to the southern slope of Luz (that is, Bethel) and went down to Ataroth-addar on the hill south of Lower Beth-horon.

On the west side the border curved southward from the hill facing Beth-horon on the south and came out at Kiriath-baal (that is, Kiriath-jearim), a city of the sons of Judah. This was the western side.
On the south side the border began at the outskirts of Kiriath-jearim and extended westward to the spring at the Waters of Nephtoah.

Then it went down to the foot of the hill that faces the Valley of Ben-hinnom at the northern end of the Valley of Rephaim and ran down the Valley of Hinnom toward the southern slope of the Jebusites and downward to En-rogel.
From there it curved northward and proceeded to En-shemesh and on to Geliloth facing the Ascent of Adummim, and continued down to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.
Then it went on to the northern slope of Beth-arabah and went down into the valley.
The border continued to the northern slope of Beth-hoglah and came out at the northern bay of the Salt Sea, at the mouth of the Jordan. This was the southern border.
On the east side the border was the Jordan. These were the borders around the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Benjamin.
These were the cities of the clans of the tribe of Benjamin: Jericho, Beth-hoglah, Emek-keziz,
Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, Bethel,
Avvim, Parah, Ophrah,
Chephar-ammoni, Ophni, and Geba, twelve cities, along with their villages.
Gibeon, Ramah, Beeroth,
Mizpeh, Chephirah, Mozah,
Rekem, Irpeel, Taralah,
Zelah, Haeleph, Jebus (that is, Jerusalem), Gibeah, and Kiriath-jearim, fourteen cities, along with their villages. This was the {{nachalah|inheritance}} of the clans of the tribe of Benjamin.

rafah.gloss: To be slack, to sink down, to let drop, to be lax. Joshua asks how long the tribes will rafah, be slack about entering and possessing the land. The word rebukes a spiritual laziness that hangs back from claiming what God has already given; the gift is sure, but it must be actively laid hold of, not neglected through complacency and delay.

goral.gloss: A lot, cast to seek God's decision. The remaining land is divided by goral in the presence of the LORD at Shiloh, so each portion is received as God's own assignment rather than human choice. Casting lots before the sanctuary joins the practical work of distribution to worship, submitting the outcome to the LORD, Proverbs 16:33.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. Benjamin's nachalah, small but wedged strategically between Judah and Joseph and holding sites like Bethel, Gibeon, and the border of Jerusalem, is received by lot as God's gift. The chapter presses that such an inheritance, freely given, is meant to be entered and possessed, not left idle.

basic.context: The nation gathers at Shiloh, and for the first time the Tent of Meeting, the center of Israel's worship, is set up in the land. But even though the land has been subdued, seven tribes still have not taken their inheritance. Joshua confronts them with a pointed question: how long will you put off possessing the land the LORD has given you? He organizes a solution: survey the remaining land, divide it into seven portions, and distribute it by lot before the LORD. The lot falls first to Benjamin, a small tribe placed strategically between Judah and Joseph, holding key sites and the border of Jerusalem. The question underneath is: why would God's people hesitate to take hold of a gift He has freely given, and what does it mean to enter fully into what is ours?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative combining a rebuke, a process of distribution, and a land record. The key spiritual point is Joshua's question about delay: the gift is sure, but it must be possessed. This becomes a rich picture of the Christian life, where believers are called to lay hold of what God has already secured for them. The setting up of the Tent of Meeting at Shiloh also marks the centrality of worship in the settled land.
basic.originalLanguages: Joshua rebukes the tribes for being rafah, slack, in possessing the land. The distribution is done by goral, by lot, before the LORD. And Benjamin receives its nachalah, its inheritance, small but strategically placed.
basic.typology: The call not to be slack but to possess what God has given pictures the believer's call to lay hold of the inheritance secured in Christ. The Tent of Meeting set up at Shiloh, God dwelling among His settled people, points to God dwelling with His people in Christ and finally in the new creation.
basic.christ: The land was given yet had to be possessed, a pattern fulfilled in Christ, who has secured every spiritual blessing for His people, which they are then called to lay hold of by faith. And the Tent of Meeting at the center of the nation points to God dwelling among His people, fulfilled in Christ, the Word who tabernacled among us, and in the church as God's dwelling by the Spirit.
basic.god: God is the generous giver who has already granted the land; the delay is the people's fault, not His. He is the God who is to be worshiped at the center of national life, His Tent of Meeting pitched at Shiloh. And He is the sovereign who assigns each portion by lot, so that the inheritance is His gift and His arrangement.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 4 warns against failing to enter God's rest through unbelief. Ephesians 1 speaks of the spiritual inheritance secured in Christ. John 1 says the Word tabernacled among us. And Philippians 3 urges pressing on to lay hold of that for which Christ laid hold of us.
basic.oneStory: At Shiloh, with the Tent of Meeting at the center, Joshua rebukes the tribes' slackness and organizes the distribution of the remaining land by lot before the LORD. The call to possess the sure gift, and the presence of God at the center of the people, point forward to Christ, in whom the inheritance is secured and God dwells among His people.

context.literary: Chapter 18 marks a shift in the allotment section. After Judah and Joseph receive their portions, the process stalls, and Joshua intervenes with a rebuke and a fresh method: survey, division into seven, and casting lots at Shiloh. This resets the distribution for the remaining seven tribes, of which Benjamin is first. The setting up of the Tent of Meeting at Shiloh, verse 1, is a significant marker, relocating Israel's worship center from Gilgal into the heart of the land. Benjamin's detailed borders and city list follow the established pattern.
context.historical: Shiloh, in the hill country of Ephraim, became the central sanctuary of Israel for the period of the judges, where the Tent of Meeting stood until the ark was later captured in the days of Eli, 1 Samuel 4. Its establishment here marks the transition from a mobile camp to a settled worship center. Benjamin, the smallest tribe, was placed between powerful Judah to the south and Joseph to the north; its territory included Bethel, Gibeon, Gibeah, and the border of Jerusalem, making it strategically and religiously significant. Benjamin would later give Israel its first king, Saul, and the New Testament apostle Paul.
context.canonical: The chapter's central rebuke, how long will you put off possessing the land, resonates through Scripture's calls not to neglect God's gifts and not to fail to enter His rest through unbelief, Hebrews 4:1-11. The establishment of the Tent of Meeting at Shiloh continues the biblical theme of God dwelling among His people, which runs from the tabernacle through the temple to the incarnation, where the Word tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and to the church as God's temple. Benjamin's territory, holding Jerusalem's border, links to the coming city of the great King.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the establishment of the Tent of Meeting at Shiloh, to preserve Joshua's rebuke of the tribes' delay in possessing their inheritance, to describe the worshipful distribution of the remaining land by lot, and to document Benjamin's allotment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The land survey and allotment describe historical events. What is prescriptive is the spiritual lesson Joshua presses: God's people are not to be slack about laying hold of what He has given, but to actively possess it. The centrality of worship, marked by the Tent of Meeting, likewise teaches that God's presence belongs at the heart of His people's life.

debates.question: Why did seven tribes delay in taking their inheritance?

views.label: Spiritual slackness and complacency
views.case: Joshua's word, how long will you put off, uses the language of slackness, suggesting a laziness or loss of resolve; with the land largely subdued, the urgency had faded, and the tribes grew content to camp rather than do the work of surveying, claiming, and settling their portions, a complacency Joshua rebukes.

views.label: Reluctance to face the remaining hard work of possession
views.case: Taking full possession meant driving out remaining Canaanites and settling difficult territory; the tribes may have shrunk from that effort, preferring the security of the camp, so their delay reflects the same reluctance to trust God for the hard parts of the conquest seen in the tribes of Joseph.

debates.bearing: Either way, Joshua treats the delay as a failure to lay hold of God's sure gift, a warning against neglecting or drifting from what God has freely given rather than actively entering it.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's force is carried by one probing word. Joshua asks the seven tribes, how long will you rafah, be slack, about entering and possessing the land the LORD has given you? The verb means to let the hands drop, to grow lax and sink down; it names a spiritual laziness that lets a sure gift lie idle. The land was already given and largely subdued, yet the tribes hung back, content to camp rather than claim their portions, and Joshua's question is a rebuke and a summons. The remedy joins work to worship: the land is surveyed and then divided by goral, by lot, in the presence of the LORD at Shiloh, so each nachalah, each inheritance, is received as God's own assignment rather than grasped by human preference. Benjamin's inheritance, small but wedged strategically between Judah and Joseph and holding Bethel, Gibeon, and the border of Jerusalem, comes up first. Slackness rebuked, lots cast before the LORD, inheritance received: the vocabulary presses that God's people must not neglect but actively lay hold of what He has freely given, and must do so in worshipful dependence on Him.

typology.type: The sure gift of land that still must be possessed
typology.fulfillment: The inheritance and blessings secured in Christ, which believers are called to lay hold of by faith rather than neglect, Ephesians 1:3 and Philippians 3:12-14.

typology.type: The Tent of Meeting set up at the center of the settled land
typology.fulfillment: God dwelling among His people, in Christ who tabernacled among us and in the church as His temple by the Spirit, John 1:14 and 1 Corinthians 3:16.

typology.type: Joshua's rebuke, how long will you put off entering
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament warning not to fail to enter God's rest through unbelief and neglect, Hebrews 4:1 and 2:3.

study.christ: Two truths in this chapter point to Christ. First, the land was already given by God, and yet the tribes had to lay hold of it; their slackness in doing so is exactly the danger the New Testament presses on believers. In Christ, God has already blessed His people with every spiritual blessing, Ephesians 1:3, and secured for them an inheritance; yet we are called to lay hold of it, to press on to make it our own, because Christ has made us His own, Philippians 3:12. Joshua's how long will you put off entering finds its echo in the warning not to neglect so great a salvation and not to fail to enter God's rest through unbelief, Hebrews 2:3 and 4:1. Second, the Tent of Meeting, God's dwelling, is now pitched at the center of the settled land, a sign that God means to dwell among His people. That longing is answered in Christ, the Word who became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and it is consummated when the dwelling of God is with man forever, Revelation 21:3. The gift is sure in Christ; the call is to enter in and dwell with the God who dwells with us.
study.god: This chapter shows God as the generous giver, the present God, and the sovereign apportioner. He is generous, having already given the land, so the tribes' failure to possess it is a fault in them, not a lack in Him; His gifts are sure. He is the present God, whose Tent of Meeting is now set up at Shiloh in the heart of the land, declaring His intention to dwell among His people and be worshiped at the center of their life. And He is the sovereign who assigns each portion by lot in His own presence, so that the inheritance is received as His arrangement, not human choosing. The God of Shiloh gives freely, dwells among His people, and orders their inheritance, and He calls them not to grow slack but to lay hold in worshipful trust of all He has provided.

crossReferences.note: Let us fear lest any fail to enter God's rest, the danger Joshua's rebuke of slackness illustrates.
God has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in Christ, an inheritance to be laid hold of by faith.
The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, fulfilling the presence signified by the Tent of Meeting.
Press on to lay hold of that for which Christ laid hold of you, the call to possess what is given.
Shiloh remained Israel's sanctuary until the ark was taken, showing its long role as the worship center established here.

study.oneStory: At Shiloh, with the Tent of Meeting now at the center of the land, Joshua rebukes the tribes for being slow to possess their God-given inheritance and organizes a worshipful distribution by lot, beginning with Benjamin. The call to lay hold of the sure gift, and the presence of God dwelling among His people, point forward to Christ, in whom the inheritance is secured and God tabernacles with His people, so that we are urged not to neglect but to enter fully into all He has given.

quiz.q: What did Joshua rebuke the seven tribes for?

options.text: For being slow to enter and possess the land the LORD had already given them.
options.note: Verse 3 records Joshua's rebuke of their delay in possessing the inheritance.

options.text: For fighting each other over the best land.
options.note: The issue was not infighting but their slackness in possessing what God had given.

options.text: For worshiping the gods of the Canaanites.
options.note: Idolatry is not the charge here; the rebuke is for delay in taking their inheritance.

options.text: For refusing to help the eastern tribes.
options.note: The eastern tribes already had their land; the rebuke concerns the seven tribes' own delay.

quiz.q: How was the remaining land distributed?

options.text: It was surveyed, divided into seven portions, and assigned by lot before the LORD at Shiloh.
options.note: Verses 4 to 10 describe the survey and casting of lots in God's presence.

options.text: Joshua simply handed each tribe whatever it asked for.
options.note: The land was carefully surveyed and assigned by lot, not given on request.

options.text: The tribes seized whatever land they could hold.
options.note: The distribution was orderly, by lot before the LORD, not a seizure.

options.text: The land was sold to the highest bidders.
options.note: It was assigned by lot as God's gift, not sold.

quiz.q: What significant thing was set up at Shiloh?

options.text: The Tent of Meeting, Israel's center of worship, now placed in the heart of the land.
options.note: Verse 1 marks the establishment of the Tent of Meeting at Shiloh.

options.text: A new fortress to defend against the Philistines.
options.note: Shiloh became a worship center, not a military fortress.
Joshua was no king with a palace; what was set up was the Tent of Meeting for worship.

options.text: A marketplace for trade among the tribes.
options.note: What was established was the sanctuary, the Tent of Meeting, not a marketplace.

quiz.q: What lasting lesson does Joshua's rebuke teach?

options.text: God's people should not be slack but actively lay hold of what He has freely given.
options.note: The gift was sure; the call was to possess it, a pattern the New Testament applies to entering God's rest.

options.text: God's gifts must always be earned by hard labor first.
options.note: The land was a gift already given; the point is possessing it by faith, not earning it.

options.text: It is fine to delay indefinitely in following God.
options.note: The opposite: Joshua rebukes delay and urges them to act now.

options.text: God had failed to provide enough land.
options.note: God had fully provided; the failure was the tribes' slackness, not God's provision.

questions: Joshua asked, how long will you put off possessing what God has given you? Where in your life are you being slack about laying hold of something God has already provided?
The land was a sure gift, yet it still had to be entered. What blessings secured for you in Christ do you need to actively lay hold of rather than leave unclaimed?
The distribution was done by lot in the presence of the LORD, joining practical work to worship. How can you bring your ordinary decisions and work more consciously before God?
The Tent of Meeting was set up at the center of the nation. Is God's presence truly at the center of your life and priorities, or pushed to the edges?
Benjamin was small but strategically placed, holding key sites for God's purposes. How does it encourage you that God uses the small and seemingly insignificant for His plans?

translationNotes.note: The Tent of Meeting set up at Shiloh marks Israel's central sanctuary for the period of the judges, until the ark was taken in the days of Eli, 1 Samuel 4.
The BSB sets off twelve cities with an em dash; this, and the similar em dash in verse 28, have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
Jebus, that is, Jerusalem, lay on Benjamin's border; the BSB footnotes note variant readings for several names in this verse. The city would not be fully taken until David, 2 Samuel 5.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 18:1-28. Prose and boundary or city lists; line-break markers within verses were ignored and segments joined with single spaces. The em dashes in verses 24 and 28 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
